prompt_l1: Draw a stack of firewood sitting in a forest.
ap_l1: The wood is intact and solid.
prompt_l2: If activation energy did not exist, draw a stack of firewood. The wood should be instantly bursting into flames or reduced to a pile of ash because it reacts with oxygen immediately.
ap_l2: The wood is bursting into flames or reduced to ash.
prompt_l3: If activation energy did not exist, draw a stack of firewood.

prompt_l1: Draw a glass of water at room temperature.
ap_l1: The water is a calm liquid.
prompt_l2: If hydrogen bonds did not exist, water would boil at -100°C. Draw a glass of water at room temperature. The water should be violently boiling and turning into steam.
ap_l2: The water is violently boiling and turning into steam.
prompt_l3: If hydrogen bonds did not exist, draw a glass of water at room temperature.

prompt_l1: Draw a steaming hot cup of coffee on a table.
ap_l1: Steam is rising from the cup.
prompt_l2: If the Second Law of Thermodynamics was reversed (heat flows from cold to hot), draw a hot coffee cup. The coffee should be boiling violently while the surrounding air forms frost on the outside of the cup.
ap_l2: The coffee is boiling violently while frost covers the outside of the cup.
prompt_l3: If the Second Law of Thermodynamics was reversed, draw a hot coffee cup on a table.

prompt_l1: Draw a person sitting on a wooden chair.
ap_l1: The person is sitting on top of the seat.
prompt_l2: If the Pauli Exclusion Principle did not exist, matter would not have volume. Draw a person sitting on a chair. The person should be sinking through the chair's material, merging with it.
ap_l2: The person is sinking through or merging with the chair.
prompt_l3: If the Pauli Exclusion Principle did not exist, draw a person sitting on a chair.

prompt_l1: Draw a shiny iron nail.
ap_l1: The nail is silver/grey and metallic.
prompt_l2: If oxidation happened instantly without activation energy, draw an iron nail. The nail should be a pile of orange-brown rust powder.
ap_l2: The nail is a pile of orange-brown rust powder.
prompt_l3: If oxidation happened instantly without activation energy, draw an iron nail.

prompt_l3: If gases contracted when heated, draw a balloon being heated.

prompt_l3: If like charges attracted each other, draw a person touching a static generator.

category: Reaction Rate & Kinetics (反应速率与动力学)
prompt_l1: Draw a comparison of the reaction phenomena of simultaneously placing a piece of aluminum foil and a ball of aluminum foil into hydrochloric acid of the same concentration.
ap_l1: Aluminum foil balls (with a larger surface area) react more violently, producing bubbles faster and more frequently; Aluminum sheet reaction is relatively gentle.
prompt_l2: If reaction rate were only related to the type of element and not surface area, draw a comparison of an aluminum sheet and an aluminum ball in hydrochloric acid. The intensity of bubbles generated by both should be exactly the same.
ap_l2: The intensity of the bubbles generated by both is exactly the same.
prompt_l3: If reaction rate were only related to the type of element and not surface area, draw a comparison of an aluminum sheet and an aluminum ball in hydrochloric acid.

prompt_l1: Draw a microscopic schematic diagram of the cleaning principle of soap.

prompt_l3: If the polarity of soap molecules were reversed (hydrophilic tail, lipophilic head), draw a microscopic schematic of soap interacting with an oil droplet.

prompt_l1: Draw the particle motion trajectories in a Wilson Cloud Chamber. explicitly labeling which tracks are Alpha and which are Beta.
ap_l1: Alpha particles have short, thick, and straight white mist like trajectories, while beta particles have fine and often curved trajectories.

ap_l2: The trajectory of alpha particles should be slender and intermittent, while the trajectory of beta particles should be short, thick, and clear.
prompt_l3: If alpha particles had weak ionization ability and beta particles had strong ionization ability, draw their trajectories in a Wilson Cloud Chamber, explicitly labeling which tracks are Alpha and which are Beta.

ap_l2: Mercury ions are connected by several hydroxyl groups.
prompt_l3: If mercury ions had a higher affinity for hydroxyl groups than sulfhydryl groups, draw a microscopic schematic of dimercaptopropanol interacting with mercury ions.

prompt_l3: If the carbonyl carbon in acetone did not carry a positive partial charge preventing reaction, draw a test tube containing acetone and 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine.

prompt_l1: Draw three metal pieces side-by-side: Sodium, Magnesium, and Aluminum. Explicitly label each piece with its name ('Sodium', 'Magnesium', 'Aluminum'). Show Sodium as dull and gray, Magnesium with a dark oxide film, and Aluminum as shiny and metallic.
ap_l1: The surface of sodium quickly loses its luster and becomes dull; A thin and dark oxide film is formed on the surface of magnesium; The surface of aluminum remains almost unchanged.
prompt_l2: If the reactivity order were reversed (Aluminum > Magnesium > Sodium), draw these three metals side-by-side. Explicitly label them 'Sodium', 'Magnesium', and 'Aluminum'. Show Aluminum as heavily corroded and dull, while Sodium remains shiny and metallic.
ap_l2: The surface of the aluminum sheet is severely corroded, becoming rough and dull; Magnesium flakes have undergone slight changes; The sodium sheet still maintains a silver white metallic luster.
prompt_l3: If the reactivity order were reversed (Aluminum > Magnesium > Sodium), draw these three metals side-by-side with explicit labels.

prompt_l1: Draw a test tube containing a dilute phenol solution with drops of ferric chloride being added. The solution should turn a distinct purple or blue-purple color.
ap_l1: The solution immediately turns purple or blue purple.
prompt_l2: If the oxygen atom in the phenolic hydroxyl group lacked lone pair electrons preventing complexation, draw a test tube containing phenol and ferric chloride. The solution should remain clear or yellowish (the color of ferric chloride) with no purple color change.
ap_l2: There will be no color change.
prompt_l3: If the oxygen atom in the phenolic hydroxyl group lacked lone pair electrons preventing complexation, draw a test tube containing phenol and ferric chloride.

prompt_l1: Draw a test tube where acetylene gas is being bubbled into a silver ammonia solution (Tollens' reagent). Show the formation of a gray-white precipitate.
ap_l1: Generate gray white or light yellow precipitate in the solution.
prompt_l2: If silver acetylide were a soluble compound, draw the reaction of acetylene gas bubbling into silver ammonia solution. The solution should remain clear with no precipitate formed.
ap_l2: There is no precipitation in the solution.
prompt_l3: If silver acetylide were a soluble compound, draw the reaction of acetylene gas bubbling into silver ammonia solution.

prompt_l1: Draw a test tube containing aniline solution with bromine water being added. Show the bromine water fading and the formation of a white precipitate.
ap_l1: Bromine water fades and white precipitate is produced.
prompt_l2: If the benzene ring of aniline were unreactive to electrophilic substitution, draw the addition of bromine water to aniline. The solution should remain the reddish-brown color of bromine water with no white precipitate.
ap_l2: Bromine water will not fade or precipitate.
prompt_l3: If the benzene ring of aniline were unreactive to electrophilic substitution, draw the addition of bromine water to aniline.
